title: Enumerative Problems Related to Moduli Spaces of Curves and Graphs
speaker: Sam Payne
abstract: I will survey new results on several enumerative problems related to the geometry, topology, and arithmetic of moduli spaces of smooth and stable curves. The enumerative problems include counting isomorphism classes of curves of genus g over a finite field Fq, and counting multiplicities of (SnxGal(Q))-representations in the cohomology of moduli spaces of smooth and stable curves.
tags: Workshop on Combinatorics of Enumerative Geometry

proof_stages.label: Finite-field curve count and polynomiality theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines N_g(q) as the number of smooth projective genus-g curves over a finite field up to geometric isomorphism, reviews low-genus formulas, and states the main theorem that N_g is polynomial exactly for g≤8.

proof_stages.label: Stable cohomology versus tautological insufficiency
proof_stages.summary: The speaker compares stabilization for Grassmannians with the Mumford-Madsen-Weiss stable cohomology of M_g, emphasizing that the tautological stable classes do not account for the large unstable cohomology indicated by Euler-characteristic growth and early examples.

proof_stages.label: High-degree unstable cohomology and graph-Lie phenomena
proof_stages.summary: Harer’s virtual cohomological dimension locates the first possible high-degree unstable classes, and known weight-zero compactly supported classes assemble into graph-complex and Lie-algebra structures with exponential growth, leaving the h^{4g-7} vanishing problem as a major open case.

proof_stages.label: Weights, stable compactification, and the boundary spectral sequence
proof_stages.summary: Deligne’s weight filtration is computed using the stable-curves compactification, whose boundary strata are indexed by stable dual graphs, producing a spectral sequence with rows built from H^j of products of smaller compactified moduli spaces modulo graph automorphisms and determinant signs.

proof_stages.label: Low rows as graph complexes and tautological rows
proof_stages.summary: The zeroth row is identified with the Kontsevich graph complex and analyzed via graph enumeration, while rows 1 and 3 vanish and rows 2 and 4 are controlled by tautological cohomology of compactified moduli spaces.

proof_stages.label: Trace formula and polynomial contributions
proof_stages.summary: Using stacky orbit counting, Serre’s comparison with coarse geometric points, and the Grothendieck-Lefschetz trace formula on the spectral sequence, the speaker shows that known even rows contribute only powers of q and that odd cohomology vanishes below degree 11, forcing polynomiality through genus 8.

proof_stages.label: First non-Tate rows and non-polynomiality from the tau motive
proof_stages.summary: The h^{11} of all compactified moduli spaces is shown to come from H^{11}(Mbar_{1,11}), where Frobenius has trace given by the Ramanujan tau function, and h^{13} handles the genus-12 exception, yielding non-polynomial point counts for all g≥9.

examples.name: Low-genus curve-count formulas
examples.note: The counts for genera 0 through 4, using objects such as the j-line, hyperelliptic branch points, plane quartics, and quadric-cubic models, make the enumerative problem concrete but do not supply the proof mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly computes the low-genus counts, deriving n0=1, n1=q, n2=q^3 and decomposing genus 3 and 4 cases into hyperelliptic/Petri/quadric contributions

examples.name: Tropical moduli space cell interpretation
examples.note: Putting lengths on stable dual graphs gives an orbi-cell model of tropical moduli space that clarifies the orientability signs in the zeroth row, but the argument can be stated directly in graph-complex terms.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker identifies the zero row with the cellular chain complex of tropical moduli space and reasons through its dual-graph cells, edge lengths, orbicell quotients, and orientability condition

passes.reason: The tropical moduli cell complex is a standard cone/orbicell interpretation of the weight-zero row, not a failure case.

examples.name: Eichler-Shimura explanation of the tau motive
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker recalls the known Eichler-Shimura/local-system explanation for the appearance of the Ramanujan tau function, which contextualizes rather than generates the non-polynomiality argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the Eichler-Shimura/tau motive connection is stated as the known explanation for the Frobenius trace tau(p), with only a brief description of the local-system source and no concrete derivation

passes.reason: The Delta/tau motive is the minimal level-one cusp-form motive and supplies non-Tate Frobenius traces obstructing polynomial point counts.
the Ramanujan Delta/tau motive is the first level-one cusp-form motive and its Frobenius traces supply the non-polynomial obstruction to polynomial curve counts

The Delta/tau motive is the first level-one cusp-form motive, and its non-Tate Frobenius traces provide the obstruction to polynomial point counts.
